creator: freebsd@byshenk.net
text:
On Tue, Jan 27, 2009 at 11:09:33AM +0000, Robert Watson wrote:
> On Tue, 27 Jan 2009, Greg Byshenk wrote:
 
> >The problem with treating EAs as "user data" and updating them as in the 
> >second format is that it breaks things in perverse ways.
> >
> >This particular issue bit me because we have secondary servers that are 
> >'rsync'ed from the primaries.  And modifying the mtime of the files when 
> >written means that the files on the secondary server are seen as 
> >"different" than those on the primary (and in need of copying), making 
> >rsync useless (as it ends up copying _every_ file, rather than the changed 
> >files).
> >
> >Additionally, I've received user complaints when they 'cp -p ...' but it 
> >does not behave as expected, as mtime and atime are _not_ preserved for 
> >files with EAs.
 
> OK, taking into account the feedback I've received so far, please find a 
> new patch attached.  The changes relative to the previous patch:
> 
> - Don't update mtime when allocating blocks or upgrading from fragments to
>   blocks for EA storage.
> - Don't update atime when accessing data from EA storage.
> 
> There's one edge case that isn't handled involving the file clustering 
> code, where IN_UPDATE is set unconditionally by ffs_reallocblks().  
> Unfortunately, this is quite hard to fix because it involves changing the 
> vnode interface and potentially affecting other file systems that use 
> clustering.  The upset is that Kostik, with whom I discussed this, and I 
> both believe that the clustering path is entirely avoided for extended 
> attribute I/O, so in practice this shouldn't result in errant mtime updates.

I've applied the patch and rebuilt, and it seems to work exactly as
expected.

I can apply and modify EAs, 'cp -p', and 'rsync', and the times appear
to be correct ('correct' here meaning: unchanged).

Thanks!

-- 
greg byshenk  -  gbyshenk@byshenk.net  -  Leiden, NL
